channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: ‘Minions’ Opens at No. 1 but Falls Far Short of Earlier Films in the Franchise
item.description: The movie generated $36.4 million in ticket sales in North America for the holiday weekend, a 62 percent decline from 2024, the last time a “Minions” movie was in theaters.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling
item.media:credit: Illumination/Universal Pictures, via Universal Pictures, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A scene from “Minions & Monsters,” beloved by critics for its ode to old Hollywood.

item.title: OPEC Plus Pledges to Pump More Even as Oil Prices Fall
item.description: The cartel’s decision comes as U.S.-Iran talks to end the war have eased pressure on oil supplies, even as broader market and political volatility persists.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.category: Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Ahmed Jadallah/Reuters
item.media:description: An oil refinery in Ras Tanura, Saudi Arabia, in 2018. The country has been actively ramping up oil shipments since the United States and Iran signed a framework agreement in June.

item.title: Taylor Swift’s Wedding Became a Marketing Moment for Brands Big and Small
item.description: Some businesses flocked to social media using A.I.-generated content, while others deployed timely digital ads designed to capitalize on the frenzy surrounding this major celebrity event.
item.dc:creator: Donna Kim and Sarah Lyon
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times
item.media:description: A “JUST&T MARRIED!” message appeared on a video screen outside Madison Square Garden on Friday evening. Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce’s wedding inspired a wave of creative marketing, from playful social media posts to digital advertisements.

item.title: I Have a Light Workload and I Simply Can’t Handle It
item.description: Plus: When a nurse practitioner in gynecology needs care, should she see one of her own colleagues?
item.dc:creator: Max Read
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by Margeaux Walter for The New York Times

item.title: Philosophers Are the Latest Hiring Target for AI Companies
item.description: A.I. labs are hiring contrarian, chin-stroking, finger-steepling sages. Who’s underemployed now?
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Wallace
item.media:credit: Aaron Wojack for The New York Times (top left); Ian C. Bates for The New York Times
item.media:description: Clockwise from top left: David Chalmers, Dillon Plunkett, Rosie Campbell and Robert Long.

item.title: Wikipedia Is Battling for the Soul of the Internet
item.description: The internet’s largest stockpile of free knowledge is under threat from MAGA, A.I. and foreign autocrats. A bibliophile ex-ambassador is here to help.
item.dc:creator: Tiffany Hsu
item.media:credit: Alex Nabaum

item.title: Nearly a Million Investors Lost a Total of $3.8 Billion on Trump Crypto Coin
item.description: A report from a cryptocurrency analytics firm details how those who bought the Trump memecoin have fared, with most retail investors having lost money while sophisticated traders did better.
item.dc:creator: Eric Lipton and David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Elizabeth Frantz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Protesters outside Trump National Golf Club in Sterling, Va., ahead of a private dinner for investors in President Donald Trump’s cryptocurrency tokens last May.

item.title: Are the ‘MANGOS’ Stocks Already Turning Soft?
item.description: Wall Street loves an acronym. The latest one stands for Meta, Anthropic, Nvidia and three other companies at the center of the artificial intelligence boom.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland
item.media:credit: Jackson Gibbs

item.title: The Rise of Racetrack Country Clubs: ‘Racing Is Golf on Steroids’
item.description: A motorsports club north of Miami, on former orange groves, is being built to cater to enthusiasts and well-heeled novices eager to hit the gas and pull some Gs.
item.dc:creator: Lawrence Ulrich
item.media:credit: Martina Tuaty for The New York Times

item.title: David S. Doty, Judge Who Helped Shape the Modern N.F.L., Dies at 96
item.description: He presided over the 1993 agreement that led to free agency and the salary cap, and for years oversaw the league’s disputes with its players.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir and Ken Belson
item.media:credit: Ben Garvin/Pioneer Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: David S. Doty in 2012. Until the year before, Judge Doty had overseen the aftermath of a 1993 class-action settlement between the N.F.L. and its players.

item.title: Why Some Banks Still Charge High Overdraft Fees
item.description: Congress scrapped a rule last year that capped how much banks could charge if you spent more than was in your account. Now, banks’ revenue from the fees is rising.
item.dc:creator: Ann Carrns

item.title: Will Late-Night TV Work on YouTube?
item.description: Julian Shapiro-Barnum, best known for a social media series called “Recess Therapy,” has started an online-only late-night show.
item.dc:creator: John Koblin and Amir Hamja
item.media:description: Julian Shapiro-Barnum filming an episode of “Outside Tonight” in the Greenpoint neighborhood of Brooklyn last month.

item.title: Why One Global Shipping Choke Point Isn’t a Blueprint for Another
item.description: The Strait of Malacca may be a model for how Oman and Iran could collect fees in the Strait of Hormuz, but the differences between the waterways are vast.
item.dc:creator: Sui-Lee Wee and Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Cheng Yiheng/Xinhua, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Some 100,000 vessels pass through the Strait of Malacca a year.

item.title: The Hottest Stock Markets Lead to the Biggest Losses
item.description: Investor enthusiasm culminated in some of the worst cases of wealth destruction in the last 100 years, a long-running study shows.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer

item.title: Egg Producers to Pay $3.3 Million to Settle Federal Price-Fixing Lawsuit
item.description: The companies also agreed to donate more than 53 million eggs to food banks and nonprofits in 17 states that joined the federal government in the antitrust action.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: Brandon Bell/Getty Images
item.media:description: The federal government began its investigation as the average wholesale price of a dozen eggs reached a high above $6 in March 2025.

item.title: Many Children Don’t Have ‘Trump Accounts.’ Some Are Missing Out on $1,000.
item.description: More than six million have been signed up for the investment accounts, which open for contributions July 4, but there are barriers to uptake.
item.dc:creator: Claire Cain Miller
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: Calling them Trump accounts has turned away some people, researchers said.

item.title: To Reduce Electrical Grid Strain Amid Heat Wave, Data Centers Are Ordered to Use Backup Power
item.description: As triple-digit temperatures engulf much of the United States, the Trump administration wants grid managers to require the use of backup power that often goes unused.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn and Hilary Howard
item.media:credit: Leah Millis/Reuters
item.media:description: The energy secretary has asked grid managers to require data centers to use backup power when electrical systems are under strain during the heat wave.

item.title: What Private Credit Is, and Why Investors Are So Worried About It
item.description: Some say the industry expanded too quickly and extended loans to companies that won’t be able to pay them back.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie for The New York Times
item.media:description: Asset managers like Blue Owl offer private credit to companies that they say traditional banks avoid.

item.title: What to know about the report.
item.dc:creator: Talmon Joseph Smith

item.title: Private Credit Can’t Stop the ‘Freak Out’
item.description: On Thursday, Blue Owl reported another quarter of significant investor withdrawal requests from some of its private credit funds.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie for The New York Times
item.media:description: Blue Owl executives have engaged in a cross-global effort over the past few months to convince backers to stay with the firm.

item.title: Tesla Sales Surge 25% on Recovery in Europe
item.description: Price cuts helped the electric automaker rebound in Europe in the second quarter, offsetting declines in the United States.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:credit: Matthias Balk/Picture Alliance via Getty Images
item.media:description: A Tesla center in Parsdorf, Germany. The automaker’s sales in the European Union were up 77 percent in the first five months of the year, a trade group said.

item.title: Why the Jobs Market Has Wall Street and Washington on Edge
item.description: A rebound in hiring has put more pressure on the Federal Reserve to raise interest rates. New jobs numbers are scheduled for release on Thursday.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin Warsh, the Fed chairman, has left investors mostly guessing about where he and the central bank stand on interest rates.

item.title: June Jobs Report Shows US Labor Market Making Slower but Steady Gains
item.description: Employers added fewer jobs in June than a month earlier but the unemployment rate ticked down, a decent showing for the U.S. economy.
item.dc:creator: Talmon Joseph Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Latest Jobs Report Shows Labor Market Is Not a Source of Inflationary Pressure
item.description: The unemployment rate ticked down to 4.2 percent, but average hourly earnings were steady, giving Kevin Warsh, the Federal Reserve chairman, room to focus on fighting inflation.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: At his first news conference as head of the Federal Reserve, Kevin Warsh said that establishing price stability would be pursued “unambiguously and unanimously.”

item.title: A.I. Is Reshaping the Economy. Good Luck Measuring How.
item.description: Some data suggest artificial intelligence is already causing job losses. Other sources show the opposite. Why is it so hard to figure out what’s going on?
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman
item.media:credit: George Wylesol

item.title: Jaguar’s Electric Future: Curves Are Out, and Blunt Is In
item.description: The storied British automaker is going all-electric, and this fall it will unveil its new flagship, with deliveries coming next year. The strategy is distinctive but risky and has drawn some prominent detractors.
item.dc:creator: Brett Berk
item.media:credit: Romain Maurice/Getty Images for Haute Living
item.media:description: The Jaguar Type 00 last year in Miami. An official unveiling is set for October.

item.title: Chanel Is Buying Charvet, the World’s Oldest Shirt Maker
item.description: The French luxury group is acquiring Charvet, a Parisian brand that dates to the 1830s, in a bid to add some men’s wear savoir-faire to its portfolio.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:credit: Randy Holmes/Disney, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Jacob Elordi wearing a Chanel x Charvet shirt for a January talk show appearance. According to Chanel’s president of fashion, Charvet’s brother-and-sister owners began mulling a sale after the success of that collaboration.

item.title: Trump Administration Delivers Lucrative Win for Its Kratom Allies
item.description: In moving to ban a potent synthetic version of kratom, the president’s team paved the way for more sales for makers of rival botanic supplements, who had aggressively lobbied for the change.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett and Kenneth P. Vogel
item.media:credit: Nick Oxford for The New York Times
item.media:description: After President Trump’s victory, companies selling kratom supplements and their executives ramped up their spending on lobbyists with ties to his administration and donations to his political operation.

item.title: Crypto Brought Trump a Huge Windfall, Even as Many Investors Lost Big
item.description: President Trump and his family reaped vast financial rewards from a memecoin that generated losses for hundreds of thousands of investors.
item.dc:creator: Eric Lipton, Andrea Fuller and David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump amassed enormous wealth during the first year of his second term, with $636 million of it coming from his cryptocurrency.

item.title: Who Can Sell ‘6 7’ Chicken Nuggets? One Company Is Asking a Court to Decide.
item.description: The meat and poultry giant Perdue said in a lawsuit that Soules Foods copied its packaging that is based on a meme popular with children.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan

item.title: Charles H. Townsend Dies at 82; Led Condé Nast During Digital Transition
item.description: By 2016, when he retired from the parent company of glossy magazines like Vogue, Vanity Fair and The New Yorker, print was seriously struggling.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter

item.title: Trump Accounts to Receive $250 Million Donation From Chip Maker Micron
item.description: President Trump said the U.S. chip maker would make a significant donation to a new type of investment account created by the administration.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Emmy Martin
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: President Trump said that Micron would invest $250 million in Trump accounts.

item.title: What Would Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce’s Prenuptial Agreement Look Like?
item.description: What would a prenuptial agreement between one of the world’s biggest pop stars and a three-time Super Bowl champion look like? Legal experts weigh in.
item.dc:creator: Aruni Soni
item.media:credit: David Eulitt/Getty Images
item.media:description: Taylor Swift has assets worth nearly $2 billion. Travis Kelce is estimated to have between $70 million and $90 million.

item.title: Fed’s Warsh Says Inflation Poses Less Risk Than a Few Weeks Ago
item.description: The Federal Reserve chairman, Kevin Warsh, stressed that the central bank would “deliver price stability,” a point he has emphasized repeatedly since taking over in May.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson and Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Pedro Rocha/Reuters
item.media:description: The Federal Reserve chairman, Kevin Warsh, left, at a forum hosted by the European Central Bank in Sintra, Portugal, on Wednesday.

item.title: Anthropic Won a Reprieve From the Trump Administration. Is It Enough?
item.description: The company can restore access to its most powerful A.I. models. But Silicon Valley remains worried about the Trump administration’s heavier hand on regulation.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount and David Meyer
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Anthropic has reached a deal with the Trump administration that would restore access to its two most potent artificial intelligence models.

item.title: As Companies Race for Cheaper A.I. Options, This Start-Up Pitches a Solution
item.description: Together AI, which specializes in open-source artificial intelligence models, is now worth more than $8 billion.
item.dc:creator: Niko Gallogly

item.title: U.S. Declines to Renew USMCA Trade Deal, Starting 10-Year Clock to Expiration
item.description: The Trump administration said Wednesday it would not immediately request to renew the North American trade agreement, leaving businesses uncertain about the trade deal’s direction.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson, Ian Austen and Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has said he might pull out of the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement.

item.title: The South Korean Mine at the Center of America’s Tungsten Push
item.description: Deep beneath a South Korean mountain, workers are reviving a mine that could challenge China’s grip on a metal vital to weapons, chips and industry.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson and Tina Hsu

item.title: Judges Strike Down Trump Rule on Loan Forgiveness for Public Servants
item.description: Two federal courts have blocked a new Trump administration rule that could have narrowed eligibility for a student loan forgiveness program for public servants.
item.dc:creator: Tara Siegel Bernard
item.media:credit: Erin Schaff/The New York Times
item.media:description: Many public servants have staked their financial futures on the loan forgiveness program. Being disqualified could have potentially upended their finances.

item.title: During Traffic Stop, N.F.L. Reporter Appeared to Show Off Her Access
item.description: A body-worn camera recording shows Dianna Russini, formerly an “insider” for The Athletic, talking football with an officer and avoiding a citation.
item.dc:creator: Katherine Rosman and Ken Belson

item.title: Trump Pulled In About $1.4 Billion From Crypto Ventures, Financial Disclosure Shows
item.description: The release of a mandatory financial disclosure for 2025 shows that the Trump family’s holdings, particularly the president’s crypto businesses, were stunningly lucrative.
item.dc:creator: Ben Protess, Andrea Fuller, Eric Lipton and David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump entering the White House on Sunday. His mandatory financial disclosure report for 2025 pulled back the curtain on his secretive business operations.

item.title: Neon Buys ‘Artificial,’ a Film About OpenAI, After Amazon Dropped It
item.description: Neon purchased “Artificial,” which focuses on OpenAI’s chief, Sam Altman, after Amazon walked away from it following an investment in the start-up.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: “Artificial,” a film about Sam Altman, the chief executive of OpenAI, had gotten a green light from Amazon in 2023.

item.title: Court Halts Pentagon Rule Requiring Escorts for Journalists
item.description: A federal judge temporarily blocked a new rule that restricted reporters’ access to the Pentagon, in a lawsuit brought by The New York Times.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The New York Times challenged the Pentagon’s rule as an unconstitutional infringement of the First Amendment.

item.title: Comcast, NBCUniversal Split Drives Deal-Making Speculation
item.description: Almost as soon as the two companies announced a split, analysts rushed to consider potential merger and acquisition targets for each.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: Comcast is planning to split off NBCUniversal into a separate company.

item.title: NPR Retracts Article That Mistakenly Said Justice Alito Would Retire
item.description: The news outlet quickly removed the article, which was written by NPR’s seasoned Supreme Court reporter, Nina Totenberg, from its website.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Justice Samuel A. Alito has not announced plans to retire.

item.title: BMW Will Build a New Electric S.U.V. in South Carolina
item.description: The German company is doubling down on electric vehicles even as other automakers pull back after acknowledging billions of dollars in losses.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: Sports Utility Vehicles and Light Trucks
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times
item.media:description: BMW’s plant in South Carolina specializes in sport utility vehicles and is the company’s largest factory outside Germany.

item.title: Nursing Students and Others Get Higher Student Loan Limits
item.description: A legal challenge to a law that takes effect this week has doubled the amount certain graduate students can borrow from the federal government to $50,000 per year.
item.dc:creator: Ron Lieber
item.media:credit: Camille Farrah Lenain for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nursing students and certain other graduate students had been limited to borrowing $20,500 per year and $100,000 total.

item.title: How the Supreme Court Ushered in Corporate Chaos in D.C.
item.description: Though the high court affirmed the political independence of the Federal Reserve, it gave the presidency huge control over dozens of federal regulators.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Lauren Hirsch, Ian Mount and Christine Zhang
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court has largely amplified presidential power, potentially making it harder for businesses to navigate Washington.

item.title: Prominent Tech Founder in Indonesia Is Found Guilty in Corruption Case
item.description: The case against Nadiem Makarim, a co-founder of Gojek, has fueled concerns about judicial fairness in a nation where foreign investors were already growing wary.
item.dc:creator: Sui-Lee Wee and Rin Hindryati
item.media:credit: Yasuyoshi Chiba/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Nadiem Makarim, a former education minister and founder of the ride-hailing app Gojek, in court in Jakarta, Indonesia, on Tuesday.

item.title: Shipping Traffic in Strait of Hormuz Picks Up Again
item.description: Vessels stranded for months have started moving in larger numbers, but many pulled back over the weekend after Iran and the U.S. exchanged attacks.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Elke Scholiers/Getty Images
